definition: A property of an object that signals how it can be interacted with.
exampleSentence: A raised button has the affordance of something that can be pressed.

definition: The structural breakdown of a UI component into its named constituent parts.
exampleSentence: The anatomy diagram breaks the card component into its labeled parts.

definition: Notes added to design files to explain decisions or specs for developer handoff.
exampleSentence: Add an annotation to explain the hover state before sending the design to dev.

definition: Design practice ensuring products are usable by people with a range of disabilities.
exampleSentence: Good accessibility means the product works for everyone, not just most people.

definition: A visual audit listing all interactive elements and their intended perceived actions.
exampleSentence: An affordance map helped us find interactive elements that weren't obvious enough.

definition: A research method where users group topics into categories that feel logical to them.
exampleSentence: Card sorting showed that users grouped settings very differently than we expected.

definition: Grouping related content together so users can process information more efficiently.
exampleSentence: Chunking the form into three steps made the process feel less overwhelming.

definition: The total mental effort required to process and interact with a user interface.
exampleSentence: Every extra option on the screen adds to the user's cognitive load.

definition: The measured difference in luminance between two colors, used to evaluate accessibility.
exampleSentence: The contrast ratio between the text and background fails the WCAG AA standard.

definition: The order in which information is presented to guide user attention and comprehension.
exampleSentence: A clear content hierarchy guides the eye from the headline to the call to action.

definition: A research method measuring emotional and aesthetic reactions to a design.
exampleSentence: Desirability testing showed users found the dark theme more professional.

definition: Accumulated inconsistencies or shortcuts in a design that need future correction.
exampleSentence: Years of design debt meant every page had a slightly different button style.

definition: The process of transferring finalized designs and specs from designer to developer.
exampleSentence: A clean design handoff means engineers don't have to guess the spacing values.

definition: A structured session where designers give and receive specific, constructive feedback.
exampleSentence: A good design critique focuses on the problem first, not the proposed solution.

definition: The ease with which users can find features or content they did not know existed.
exampleSentence: Low discoverability means users find the feature by accident, not by looking.

definition: An unusual or extreme scenario that falls outside typical user behavior patterns.
exampleSentence: What does the empty state look like? That's an important edge case to design.

definition: The UI state shown when there is no content to display, often used to guide users.
exampleSentence: The empty state should explain what goes here and how to add the first item.

definition: A UI condition that communicates something went wrong and guides the user to recover.
exampleSentence: Every form field needs an error state that tells the user exactly what's wrong.

definition: The level of detail and realism of a prototype, ranging from low to high.
exampleSentence: Start with low fidelity to explore ideas quickly before committing to details.

definition: A model predicting that target acquisition time depends on the target's size and distance.
exampleSentence: Fitts's law explains why the submit button should be large and close to the form.

definition: The sequence of steps a user takes to complete a task within a product.
exampleSentence: Map the flow from landing page to checkout before designing any single screen.

definition: Perceptual principles describing how the brain organizes visual elements into unified wholes.
exampleSentence: Gestalt principles explain why elements close together feel like they belong together.

definition: A button with a transparent fill and only an outline border, typically a secondary action.
exampleSentence: Use a ghost button for secondary actions so the primary action stands out more.

definition: A mathematical proportion (~1.618) applied in design to achieve visually balanced layouts.
exampleSentence: The golden ratio can guide proportions in a layout to feel naturally balanced.

definition: A structure of columns, rows, and gutters used to align and organize interface elements.
exampleSentence: The twelve-column grid system keeps every layout consistent across the product.

definition: Informal usability testing conducted quickly in public spaces with random participants.
exampleSentence: Guerrilla testing in a coffee shop gave us five insights in under an hour.

definition: A practical usability principle used to evaluate and identify interface problems.
exampleSentence: Jakob Nielsen's ten heuristics are a good starting point for any UI review.

definition: An expert review of a UI measured against established usability principles.
exampleSentence: A heuristic evaluation found three serious usability problems before any user testing.

definition: Visual structure that signals the relative importance of elements to guide user attention.
exampleSentence: Strong visual hierarchy draws the eye to the most important action on the page.

definition: A defined clickable area in a prototype that triggers navigation or an interaction.
exampleSentence: Add a hotspot to the menu icon so testers can navigate in the prototype.

definition: The structural design of shared information environments to support usability and findability.
exampleSentence: Poor information architecture was why users couldn't find their account settings.

definition: The practice of designing how users interact with digital products over time.
exampleSentence: Interaction design defines how the panel animates in and what triggers it.

definition: A cyclic design process of prototyping, testing, and refining based on user feedback.
exampleSentence: Iterative design got us from a rough idea to a tested solution in two weeks.

definition: A framework focusing on the underlying goal a user is trying to accomplish with a product.
exampleSentence: The jobs-to-be-done framework helped us see that users hire the app to save time.

definition: A visualization of the end-to-end experience a user has with a product over time.
exampleSentence: The journey map revealed a frustrating gap between signup and the first real value.

definition: How quickly a new user can become proficient at using an interface.
exampleSentence: High learnability means users can do basic tasks correctly on their first try.

definition: A user's internal representation of how a system works based on prior experience.
exampleSentence: Match the UI to the user's mental model of how folders and files should work.

definition: A small, contained product moment that accomplishes a single task with user feedback.
exampleSentence: A small microinteraction on the save button confirms the action without interrupting.

definition: A dialog overlay that requires user interaction before returning to the main interface.
exampleSentence: Use a modal for destructive actions so the user must consciously confirm.

definition: Minimum Viable Product — a version with just enough features to test a core hypothesis.
exampleSentence: The MVP had only three features but was enough to validate the core idea.

definition: A reusable structural approach for helping users move through an interface.
exampleSentence: A tab bar is the right navigation pattern for apps with four to five main sections.

definition: The process of guiding new users to understand and adopt a product's core value.
exampleSentence: Smooth onboarding is the single biggest predictor of long-term retention.

definition: A specific problem or frustration a user experiences when interacting with a product.
exampleSentence: The biggest pain point users described was having to re-enter the same data twice.

definition: A design approach that actively involves users as co-creators in the design process.
exampleSentence: Participatory design sessions gave users a voice in shaping the new dashboard.

definition: A fictional but research-based character representing a key user segment of a product.
exampleSentence: The primary persona is a project manager who checks the tool three times a day.

definition: Revealing information gradually to reduce complexity and avoid overwhelming the user.
exampleSentence: Progressive disclosure hides advanced settings until the user asks for them.

definition: A simulation of a product used to test ideas before committing to development.
exampleSentence: We built a clickable prototype before writing a single line of production code.

definition: A gestalt principle stating that elements placed close together are perceived as related.
exampleSentence: Proximity groups the label and input so the relationship is immediately clear.

definition: The critical tasks users perform most frequently that must work flawlessly in a product.
exampleSentence: The checkout is a red route — nothing should block or slow it down.

definition: A design approach where layouts adapt fluidly to different screen sizes and devices.
exampleSentence: Responsive design means the layout adapts gracefully from desktop to phone.

definition: Temporary UI support structures that guide users until they no longer need assistance.
exampleSentence: The scaffold guides new users until they're comfortable enough to explore on their own.

definition: The ease with which users can quickly read and extract key information from a page.
exampleSentence: Good scannability means users find what they're looking for without reading everything.

definition: How easily a product can be maintained, updated, or corrected after deployment.
exampleSentence: High serviceability means the team can patch and update the product quickly.

definition: A visual or auditory cue that communicates how an object or element should be used.
exampleSentence: The underline on a link is a signifier telling users they can click it.

definition: A placeholder UI that mimics the layout of content while data is loading.
exampleSentence: A skeleton screen keeps the layout stable while the content is still loading.

definition: A design style where digital elements mimic the appearance of real-world counterparts.
exampleSentence: Skeuomorphism made the early iPhone apps look like real-world objects on purpose.

definition: A UI element that remains fixed in the viewport as the user scrolls the page.
exampleSentence: The sticky element keeps the navigation bar visible as the user scrolls down.

definition: A linear sequence of steps a user follows to complete one specific task.
exampleSentence: Map the task flow for resetting a password before designing any single screen.

definition: A usability method where participants verbalize their thoughts while using a product.
exampleSentence: The think-aloud protocol revealed that users were confused by the icon's meaning.

definition: A brief, non-blocking notification that appears temporarily to confirm an action.
exampleSentence: A toast message in the corner confirms the file was saved without interrupting the user.

definition: A small label that appears on hover to provide additional context for an element.
exampleSentence: A tooltip explains what the icon button does when the user hovers over it.

definition: A research method evaluating how easily users find items within a navigation structure.
exampleSentence: Tree testing showed that most users looked for billing under Account, not Settings.

definition: Observing real users attempting tasks to identify friction points in a product.
exampleSentence: Five sessions of usability testing surfaced the three most critical problems.

definition: A diagram showing all possible paths a user can take through a product.
exampleSentence: The user flow diagram maps every path from signup to the first successful action.

definition: A short description of a feature from the user's perspective: As a… I want… So that…
exampleSentence: Write a user story for each feature so the team knows who it's for and why.

definition: The degree to which a product provides features that fulfill real user needs.
exampleSentence: A feature with low utility that is hard to use will not survive the next redesign.

definition: Real-time feedback confirming that a user's input meets the required format or criteria.
exampleSentence: Inline validation tells users about a problem before they submit the whole form.

definition: The perceived heaviness of a design element based on size, color, contrast, and position.
exampleSentence: The primary button has more visual weight than the secondary one to stand out.

definition: Design strategies that help users understand where they are and how to navigate a system.
exampleSentence: Breadcrumbs are a classic wayfinding tool that shows users where they are.

definition: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — international standards for accessible digital content.
exampleSentence: Every color combination in the UI needs to pass the WCAG AA contrast ratio.

definition: Empty space in a layout used intentionally to improve readability and visual balance.
exampleSentence: More whitespace around the headline made the page feel cleaner and more focused.

definition: A low-fidelity schematic showing the structure and layout of a screen without visual design.
exampleSentence: A wireframe focuses on layout and content without getting distracted by color.

definition: A multi-step UI pattern that breaks a complex task into sequential, manageable steps.
exampleSentence: A wizard breaks the complex account setup into six manageable steps.

definition: The first-time experience when a user has no data yet — often an onboarding opportunity.
exampleSentence: The zero state is the perfect moment to guide users to their first action.

definition: A reading pattern where eyes scan across the top, diagonally down, then across the bottom.
exampleSentence: The hero section uses a Z-pattern layout to lead the eye toward the CTA.

definition: A common reading pattern where users scan horizontally at top, then skim down the left side.
exampleSentence: F-pattern reading means the most important content should sit on the left side.

definition: The portion of a webpage visible without scrolling, considered prime real estate.
exampleSentence: Put the main call to action above the fold so it's visible without scrolling.

definition: A secondary navigation trail showing the user's current location within a site hierarchy.
exampleSentence: A breadcrumb trail helps users navigate back from a deep page in the hierarchy.

definition: A UI element that prompts users to take a specific desired action, often a button.
exampleSentence: The call to action should be the most visually prominent element on the page.

definition: A deceptive UI design that tricks users into actions they did not intend to take.
exampleSentence: A pre-checked newsletter box is a common dark pattern in signup flows.

definition: A cycle where system responses to user actions inform and shape subsequent behavior.
exampleSentence: A short feedback loop between users and the team keeps the product on the right track.
